nuggets.summary: Sandler cites Stanford professor Noam Wasserman's book "The Founder's Dilemmas," which studied hundreds of founding teams over nine years and asked both successful and unsuccessful ones why they turned out the way they did. Roughly two-thirds of the time, founders attributed the outcome to team-related issues rather than market, product, or execution problems — things like lack of shared vision, disagreement on go-to-market strategy, or an equity split that didn't incentivize the right people correctly. The non-obvious takeaway: in early-stage companies, the dominant failure mode isn't strategic or competitive, it's interpersonal — which argues for spending as much diligence on co-founder fit and equity structure as on the market opportunity itself.
nuggets.quote: what he found was that something on the order of two-thirds of the time, those founders would point to team-related issues. Whether it wasn't shared vision, whether it was, you know, disagreement around a go-to-market strategy, or even whether we didn't split the equity in a way that allowed for the right incentives to play out.

nuggets.summary: Sandler pushes back on the reflexive founder assumption that raising venture capital is a default milestone on the path to building a company. She says most founders she meets never actually ask themselves whether they want VC in the first place, and that for most companies, venture is not the best form of funding — it comes with structural pressure to grow fast ("put the pedal to the metal") and a term sheet with far more control and economic provisions attached than an angel round. Her rule of thumb: a business needs a market opportunity meaningfully larger than $500 million to even be interesting to a fund like Madrona's, and if you're building something closer to a lifestyle business, raising venture doesn't make sense regardless of how prestigious it feels to have a name-brand investor.
nuggets.quote: the fact is, most companies, venture is not the best form of funding... if you're building a business that you want to, you know, complement your, your life or like a lifestyle business as they call it, it's, doesn't make sense to raise venture capital.

nuggets.summary: Sandler explains that Madrona's most recent fund at the time was $300 million, and because a fund that size can only make a limited, deliberate number of investments, each one carries a real opportunity cost — meaning the underlying market opportunity has to be large enough to potentially move the needle on the whole fund. She states explicitly that even a $500 million market opportunity, which sounds large to most founders, is "not that compelling" to VCs at that fund size. The actionable implication for founders is to size their pitch to the fund: know roughly how large a fund is targeting you and back into whether your addressable market is actually big enough to justify their model, rather than assuming any market opportunity that sounds big to you will read the same way to an institutional VC.
nuggets.quote: we're not going to be interested if a company is, has a, you know, a market opportunity of, you know, $500 million. That's a really big market opportunity to go after. But even that, it's not as compelling to, to VCs.

nuggets.summary: Asked how much money founders should raise, Sandler describes a specific backward-planning method rather than a rule of thumb: start from what you need to accomplish in the next 18–24 months (a defined, meaningful milestone — a product launch, a working prototype, a growth rate), then itemize what it takes to get there (headcount, G&A, marketing spend, working capital), and only then back into the raise number. She flags that if the resulting number is something like $100 million, that's usually a sign the math is wrong. The value of this exercise isn't just arriving at a number — she says it also produces a more productive investor conversation, because you can defend both the milestone and the spend that gets you there, rather than just naming a round size that feels standard.
nuggets.quote: think about what is it that you want to accomplish in the next 18 months... And then think, okay, what do I need to do to get there? Who do I need to hire? What do I need to invest in... And then think, okay, how much should I be raising now to achieve that.

nuggets.summary: Sandler names the specific metrics she says actually matter when evaluating an early-stage company, as distinct from the many that don't. The core one is quantifiable customer reaction — capturing the funnel from an email address, to a free version, to a paid version — paired with understanding cost per acquired customer against lifetime value (LTV), which she says is preferred over lifetime revenue because it accounts for gross margin. Alongside that, she says payback period (how many months until an acquired customer's value covers what it cost to acquire them) is a number worth building reporting for from day one, not something to bolt on later once investors ask for it.
nuggets.quote: if you're paying X dollars to acquire one customer, how much value... you're extracting from that customer? Whether it's value in the first 12 months, the first two years, whether it's value in a lifetime. That metric is classically called, you know, LTV, lifetime value... understanding how quickly you achieve payback on that acquisition... that kind of stuff matters a lot.

nuggets.summary: Sandler advises founders to run diligence on their prospective investor with the same rigor a VC runs on them, because the relationship is symmetric and long — she notes a Madrona-portfolio company typically stays under a VC's umbrella for five to seven years, which she points out is longer than most U.S. marriages. Her concrete suggestion is to talk to other CEOs who have worked with that investor and ask hard questions specifically about how that investor behaves in bad moments, not just good ones — since the value of a VC relationship shows up most when a company hits a rough patch and needs an investor who tells (and hears) bad news early rather than one who disappears or turns adversarial.
nuggets.quote: roughly we see that it's about five to seven years or so that a company will stay in our portfolio, which is a really long time. In fact, that is longer than most U.S. marriages these days... a really important exercise that each of you should be going through when you raise capital is diligence on the investor.

nuggets.summary: Sandler identifies a specific, common mistake she sees in early founders: not talking to enough people before committing to a direction. She distinguishes this from customer discovery — it's specifically about talking to smart people whose judgment you respect, who can give critical feedback while an idea is still being shaped (business model, go-to-market, what opportunity to actually pursue). Her framing of how to use that feedback is itself a nuance: you don't adopt criticism wholesale, you extract what's relevant and let it sharpen your own perspective and pitch — treating a wide net of blunt conversations as a low-cost way to pressure-test an idea before it's expensive to change.
nuggets.quote: I think another mistake that entrepreneurs make is just not talking to enough people... talking to smart people you respect, whose opinion you respect, who can give you critical feedback... You don't necessarily... adopt... their feedback or their criticism a hundred percent. You take out what's going to be relevant and meaningful to you.

nuggets.summary: Asked what's hardest for young, inexperienced entrepreneurs to learn, Sandler flips the framing: she argues naivete is actually an advantage, not a deficiency, because founders who haven't been burned by a market or watched prior attempts fail aren't boxed in by the belief that certain doors are already closed. As people gain experience, she says, it gets harder to think outside the paradigm they've been operating in — meaning the conventional worry (young founders lack scar tissue and pattern recognition) is, in her view, outweighed by the upside of not yet being conditioned to see opportunities as impossible.
nuggets.quote: young entrepreneurs I'd say, I mean, are often the strongest because they aren't as jaded by particular markets and competitive landscapes that others are. So I think in anything, naivete almost can be a huge advantage because you just, you see opportunity... as you get more experience, it's, um, it's harder to think outside of the paradigm that you've been operating in.

nuggets.summary: Sandler describes the Seattle-area talent gap in concrete terms: demand for University of Washington computer science degree slots runs at roughly four times the supply the department can offer to qualified, talented applicants. Her point is that this isn't fixable through education pipeline investment alone — even if CS were taught in every classroom and UW could fully scale its program, local supply still couldn't keep pace with demand from fast-growing companies. The actionable conclusion she draws is a policy one: the gap has to be closed by importing technical talent, which is why she specifically calls out raising the H-1B visa threshold and loosening non-compete restrictions (citing Massachusetts as a model) as necessary, not optional, fixes for a region's startup ecosystem.
nuggets.quote: talk with them about the demand for computer science degrees and the amount of spots that they have to offer students who are completely qualified, extremely talented... I think it's, you know, 4X the amount of demand is they have to supply of spots.
